answer:
My key health habit is definitely regular exercise. It's something I've made a priority in my daily routine, and it's had a huge impact on my overall well-being. I was inspired to start this habit by my older brother. He's always been really active and fit, and seeing how much energy and positivity he has made me want to follow in his footsteps.

Regular exercise matters to me because it's not just about staying in shape; it's about feeling good from the inside out. It gives me a boost of energy, helps me manage stress, and improves my mood. Plus, it's a great way to start the day or unwind after a long day at work. I've noticed that when I exercise regularly, I sleep better, have more focus, and just feel more resilient overall.

One of the reasons it sticks with me is because I've found ways to make it enjoyable. I mix it up with different activities—sometimes I go for a run, other times I do yoga or hit the gym. Keeping it varied helps keep it interesting and prevents burnout.

In the end, this habit has become a cornerstone of my daily routine, and it's something I'm really grateful for. It's not just about physical health; it's about mental and emotional well-being too. And having my brother as a role model has been a huge motivator.
title: What's your key health habit? Who inspired it? Why does it matter?

answer:
Showing people you care about them can take many forms, and it really depends on the person and the relationship. For me, one of the most important ways is through quality time. I make an effort to spend meaningful moments with the people I care about, whether it's having a deep conversation over coffee or just hanging out and enjoying each other's company. It's about being present and showing that I value the time we share.

Another way I show care is through small gestures. It could be as simple as sending a thoughtful message to check in, bringing a friend their favorite snack, or helping out with a task they've been struggling with. These little acts of kindness can go a long way in showing someone that you're thinking of them and that you care.

As for how others show they care about me, it's often through similar actions. I've had friends who've gone out of their way to surprise me with something I love or who've listened intently when I needed to talk. It's the genuine concern and the willingness to be there that really speaks volumes. When someone takes the time to understand my feelings and support me, it makes me feel valued and loved.

In the end, showing care is about being there for each other, in good times and bad. It's about creating a sense of trust and connection, and it's something that can strengthen relationships and bring people closer together.
title: How do you show people you care about them? How do others show they care about you?

answer:
The device I use most often each day is definitely my smartphone. It's like the Swiss Army knife of modern life—super versatile and always within reach. I use it for everything from checking emails and staying connected with friends and family through social media and messaging apps, to using it as a mini-computer for quick searches and even as a camera to capture spontaneous moments.

The reason I use it so much is that it's incredibly convenient. It's like having a personal assistant in my pocket. I can manage my schedule, set reminders, and even do some light work on the go. Plus, it's my primary source of entertainment when I'm waiting in line or commuting. I can listen to music, watch videos, or read articles to pass the time.

If I had to go a week without my smartphone, my daily life would definitely feel a bit off-kilter. I'd have to rely more on my computer for work-related tasks, which means I'd need to be near a power outlet and a desk. Staying in touch with people would be a bit more challenging—I'd probably have to make more calls from a landline or use other means of communication. I'd also miss having a portable camera, so capturing spontaneous moments would be harder.

On the flip side, I might find myself being more present in the moment, engaging more in face-to-face conversations, and maybe even reading a physical book or two. It could be a good reminder to take a break from the digital world and reconnect with the analog one. But overall, my smartphone is such a fundamental part of my daily routine that going without it for a week would definitely require some adjustments.

answer:
Well, one thing that's definitely on my shopping list this year is a new laptop. My current one is getting pretty old and slow, and it's starting to affect my productivity. A new laptop would make a huge difference in my daily life. I could work more efficiently, enjoy smoother video calls, and even have a better experience when I'm catching up on my favorite shows or movies. Plus, with a faster and more reliable device, I'd be able to take on more projects and stay connected with friends and family more easily.

As for how I plan to afford it, I've been setting aside a little bit of money each month. It's like creating a mini savings fund specifically for this purchase. I've also been looking for deals and discounts, keeping an eye out for sales during holiday seasons or special promotions. And if I can find a good refurbished option that meets my needs, I'd definitely consider that too—it's a great way to save money while still getting a high-quality product.

In the meantime, I'm also trying to be more mindful of my spending in other areas. Cutting back on non-essential expenses here and there can really add up, and it'll help me reach my goal faster. So, with a bit of planning and some smart shopping, I'm confident I'll be able to get that new laptop and make my daily life a little bit better.

answer:
I think whether sports players should get paid lots of money is a bit of a double-edged sword. On one hand, I get it—they're entertainers, and they bring a ton of joy and excitement to fans. They work incredibly hard, training for hours every day, and they deserve to be rewarded for their talent and dedication. Plus, professional sports generate a lot of money, so it makes sense that the players should get a fair share.

Take, for example, the NFL or the NBA. These players are like modern-day gladiators, giving it their all on the field or court. They bring communities together, and their performances can be truly inspiring. Paying them well is a way of recognizing their value and the impact they have.

On the other hand, there's an argument to be made that the salaries can be a bit excessive. There are other professions, like teachers or healthcare workers, who are doing incredibly important work but aren't compensated nearly as much. It can feel a bit off when you see a player making millions while others who are equally dedicated struggle to make ends meet.

But at the end of the day, I think it's important to recognize the unique demands of being a professional athlete. The physical toll, the pressure, and the short career span all justify the high pay. It's a tough, competitive world, and those who make it to the top deserve to be rewarded for their efforts.
title: Should sports players get paid lots of money? Why or why not?

answer:
Oh, I remember this one time in high school when I didn't agree with my history teacher about a particular interpretation of an event. We were discussing the causes of World War I, and I had a different perspective based on some research I had done outside of class. I felt pretty strongly about it, but I also knew I needed to handle it respectfully.

So, instead of arguing in front of the whole class, I asked if I could speak with the teacher after class. I explained my point of view and showed her some of the sources I had found. She listened, and even though she didn't change her stance, she appreciated that I had done my homework and was willing to discuss it calmly.

This experience taught me a lot about how to deal with teachers better. First, it showed me the importance of being respectful and professional, even when you disagree. Second, it highlighted the value of being prepared with evidence and reasoning. Finally, it made me realize that teachers are human too, and they appreciate when students engage thoughtfully.

From then on, I felt more confident about sharing my opinions in class, but I also knew how to do it in a way that was constructive and respectful. It definitely improved my relationship with that teacher and made me a better student overall.

answer:
New technology has really transformed how we have fun and interact with others. For example, social media platforms like Facebook and Instagram have made it super easy to stay connected with friends and family, no matter where they are. I can just pull out my phone and see what everyone's up to, or send a quick message to catch up. It's like having a global chat room in your pocket.

When it comes to having fun, technology has opened up a whole new world of possibilities. Video games, for instance, have evolved so much. Now, you can play with friends online, even if they're on the other side of the planet. It's like being in the same room, sharing laughs and challenges together. And with virtual reality (VR) on the horizon, the future of gaming looks even more exciting.

But it's not just about staying connected or having fun. Technology has also changed the way we communicate in more profound ways. For example, during the pandemic, video calls through Zoom and FaceTime became a lifeline for many people. We could still have birthday parties, family gatherings, and even work meetings without being in the same physical space. It was a bit like magic, bringing people together when we couldn't be together in person.

However, there are some downsides. Sometimes, all this technology can make us feel a bit isolated. Even though we're "connected," it's not the same as a face-to-face conversation. And with so many notifications and distractions, it can be hard to focus on the people right in front of us. But overall, I think the benefits far outweigh the drawbacks. Technology has made our lives so much richer and more connected, and I'm excited to see what the future holds.
title: How has new technology changed how we have fun or talk to others? Share your experience.

answer:
So, I recently gave my parents a gift that I thought was pretty special. I chose a personalized photo album filled with pictures from our family trips over the years. You know, those moments we shared, from camping trips to holidays, all captured in one place.

When I gave it to them, their reaction was just heartwarming. My mom teared up a bit, which is always a sign that she's really touched. She started flipping through the pages, reminiscing about all the good times we had. My dad, he was more reserved, but I could see the smile on his face. He said it was one of the best gifts he'd ever received because it brought back so many memories.

It was really nice to see them both so happy. It made me realize that sometimes, the simplest gifts can mean the most. It wasn't about the cost or anything fancy; it was about the thought and the memories. It was a way for me to say, "I love you," and to remind them of all the beautiful moments we've shared as a family.
title: Tell me about a gift you recently gave your parents. What did you choose? How did they react?

answer:
Oh, I see there's no specific prompt given here. But, let's imagine I have to talk about a recent hobby I've picked up, so I’ll go ahead and describe that.

So, recently, I started getting into baking, which has been quite the adventure, to say the least. You know, I always thought baking was just whipping up some cookies or a cake, but it's, like, way more intricate than that. I mean, there’s a lot of science behind it—measuring ingredients precisely, understanding how different types of flour affect the texture of your dough, and all those little things that can make or break your recipe.

At first, I had a few... let’s call them "learning experiences," where my creations didn't turn out exactly as planned. I made this loaf of bread once that was, well, more like a brick than anything you’d want to eat. But hey, practice makes perfect, right?

Now, after a few weeks, I’ve gotten better and can actually make some pretty tasty stuff. It’s really satisfying to see your ingredients transform into something delicious. Plus, it's a great way to unwind and take a break from screens. So, yeah, baking has definitely become my new favorite pastime, and I’m excited to keep learning and experimenting with different recipes.
title: How do you plan what to do each day or week?

answer:
One thing I've really tried hard to get better at is public speaking. It's something that intimidates a lot of people, and it definitely got me nervous at first. But I realized that being good at it could open up so many opportunities, whether it's giving presentations at work or just being more confident when speaking up in a group.

The reason I want to be good at it is pretty straightforward: communication is key in almost every aspect of life. If I can articulate my thoughts clearly and confidently, it not only helps in my career but also in my personal relationships. Plus, it's a skill that's always in demand, and it feels empowering to know you can hold your own in any situation.

When it gets tough, which it definitely does, I remind myself why I started in the first place. I practice a lot, which helps build my confidence. I also try to learn from every experience, whether it's a small presentation or a big speech. After each one, I think about what went well and what I could improve. And sometimes, I just have to push through the nerves and tell myself that it's okay to make mistakes—that's how we grow.

So, even though it's challenging, I keep at it because I know the payoff is worth it. It's like running a marathon—you just have to keep putting one foot in front of the other, and eventually, you'll reach the finish line.
title: What have you tried really hard to get better at? Why do you want to be good at it? How do you keep trying when it's hard?

answer:
One skill that I want to learn is digital marketing. In today's technology-driven world, having knowledge and skills in digital marketing has become crucial for businesses and individuals alike. Digital marketing involves promoting products or services using digital channels such as social media, search engines, email marketing, and content marketing. Learning digital marketing would provide me with the ability to create effective marketing campaigns, analyze data and trends, and reach a wider audience.

With this skill, I would be able to optimize websites for search engines, create engaging content for social media platforms, and develop email marketing strategies. Moreover, digital marketing offers a wide range of career opportunities in various industries and allows individuals to work remotely.

To learn digital marketing, I would start by acquiring a solid understanding of the basic concepts and principles. Then, I would explore different online courses and resources to enhance my knowledge and gain practical experience. Continuous learning and staying updated with the latest trends and tools in digital marketing will be essential to succeed in this field.
title: Describe a skill you want to learn.

answer:
Oh man, I've got to tell you about the time I visited the Grand Canyon. Before I went, I was just a bundle of nerves and excitement. I mean, it's one of those places you see in all the travel magazines and on TV, and I was dying to see it for myself. I was like a kid on Christmas Eve, couldn't sleep the night before.
When I finally got there, it was like, whoa! The view was just mind-blowing. I remember standing on the edge, looking out over this massive chasm that seemed to go on forever. The colors were incredible—reds, oranges, and browns blending together in this crazy tapestry. And the scale of it all, it was just so vast that it made me feel tiny, you know?
What really got me though was the silence. Despite all the tourists, there was this peaceful quiet that let you really take in the grandeur of it all. It was like time stood still. I could have stood there for hours just taking it all in. It was definitely a moment I'll never forget, and it made me appreciate the beauty of nature in a whole new way.

answer: Okay, so one trending thing I've noticed recently is the rise of sustainable fashion. You know, like brands and people are trying to focus more on eco-friendly clothing, reusing materials, or even thrifting. I think it’s popular because, well, people are becoming more aware of environmental issues and want to make choices that align with those values. Plus, it’s kind of cool—like, wearing thrifted clothes or upcycled pieces makes you stand out in a creative way. Honestly, it impacts people by encouraging a shift away from fast fashion, which can be super wasteful, and also gets them to think more critically about what they buy. For example, I’ve seen my friends getting into thrifting, and it’s actually helped them save money while looking unique. So yeah, it’s a pretty positive trend overall.

answer:
The best movie I've seen has to be "The Shawshank Redemption." It's one of those films that stays with you long after the credits roll. What made it special was the way it blended hope, friendship, and resilience into a story that felt both heart-wrenching and uplifting.

One of the things I loved most about the movie was its pacing. It took its time to develop the characters and their relationships, especially the bond between Andy Dufresne and Ellis "Red" Redding. Their friendship was the backbone of the story, and it made every twist and turn feel deeply personal.

The part I liked most was the ending. It was like a perfect storm of emotion—Andy's escape, the reveal of his plan, and Red's eventual reunion with him in Zihuatanejo. It was a payoff that felt earned after the long journey. The final shot of Red and Andy on the beach was like a breath of fresh air, a moment of pure joy after so much hardship.

What made "The Shawshank Redemption" truly special was its ability to capture the human spirit. It showed that even in the darkest of places, hope can shine through. It's a movie that reminds you to keep pushing forward, no matter what life throws at you.

answer:
I recently attended a talk by a local environmental activist named Sarah. She spoke about the importance of reducing plastic waste in our daily lives. She shared some really eye-opening facts about how much plastic ends up in the ocean and the impact it has on wildlife. She also gave practical tips on how we can all make small changes, like using reusable bags and bottles.
I really liked the talk. Sarah was super engaging and made a potentially dry topic feel urgent and important. She had this way of making everyone in the room feel like they could make a difference. Her passion was contagious, and she left us with actionable steps that felt doable. It was one of those talks that made me want to go home and start making some changes right away. It was inspiring and left me feeling hopeful about what we can achieve if we all pitch in.

answer:
I think one of the most significant medical discoveries that changed the world is the development of antibiotics. Penicillin, discovered by Alexander Fleming in 1928, completely revolutionized medicine. Before antibiotics, even minor infections could be deadly. But with penicillin, doctors gained a powerful tool to treat bacterial infections, saving countless lives.
What made it so significant was its impact on public health. It allowed for safer surgeries, reduced mortality rates, and even helped in treating diseases that were once considered untreatable. During World War II, penicillin was mass-produced and used to treat wounded soldiers, saving many lives. This discovery also paved the way for further advancements in medicine, making it easier to combat infections and improve overall health outcomes.
In short, antibiotics like penicillin have been a game-changer in modern medicine, transforming how we treat infections and improving global health.
title: Which medical discovery changed the world most? How did it help people? Why was it so significant?

answer:
Oh, I've got a doozy of a happy memory from my childhood that still puts a smile on my face. It was the summer I was about eight years old, and my family took a trip to the beach. Now, I had never seen the ocean before, and it was like stepping into a whole new world. The sand between my toes, the salty air, and the endless blue horizon—it was magical.
What makes this memory so special is the sense of wonder and freedom I felt. I remember building the most epic sandcastle, complete with moats and towers. I was so proud of it, and when the waves finally washed it away, I wasn't sad. It was like a lesson in impermanence, but in the most beautiful way.
As for who else knows this story, well, my family loves to reminisce about it. My mom always says I was the "sandcastle king" that summer, and my dad still chuckles when he remembers how I tried to save my castle from the tide. They think it's a cute story, a snapshot of a carefree time when life was simple and full of firsts.
It's a memory that's been passed down, shared around the dinner table, and it always brings a round of smiles and laughter. It's like a little time capsule that captures the joy and innocence of childhood, and it's a story that I'll always treasure.
title: Tell me about a happy memory from your childhood. Why is it special? Who else knows this story, and what did they think?

answer:
The greatest accomplishment of my life is establishing a non-profit organization dedicated to providing education and support to underprivileged children. This organization, called "Brighter Futures," aims to create equal opportunities for all children, regardless of their socioeconomic backgrounds.

To achieve this, I started by conducting extensive research on the educational needs of underprivileged communities. I collaborated with local governments, schools, and community leaders to identify the areas that required the most attention. By understanding the specific challenges faced by these children, I was able to design targeted programs and interventions.

Next, I mobilized a team of passionate volunteers who shared the same vision. Together, we organized fundraising events, reached out to potential sponsors, and secured partnerships with local businesses. These efforts allowed us to gather the necessary resources to provide educational materials, scholarships, and mentorship programs for the children in need.

Additionally, we partnered with local schools to implement after-school programs that focused on academic support, life skills development, and extracurricular activities. By working closely with the students and their families, we were able to create a supportive environment that fostered personal growth and encouraged a love for learning.

Over time, Brighter Futures has expanded its reach and impact, positively influencing the lives of countless children. Witnessing the transformation of these young individuals, who now have access to education and brighter opportunities, is truly the greatest accomplishment of my life.
title: What is the greatest accomplishment of your life? How did you achieve it?

Okay, so one rule I have to follow at work is to always be on time for meetings. I mean, it's pretty straightforward, but it really does carry a lot of weight. The purpose of this rule is basically to respect everyone's time and keep things running smoothly. When you're in a meeting, especially if it's a team effort, you want to make sure everyone is there to contribute and share ideas, right? 

So, this rule applies to everyone in the office—my colleagues, our managers, even the higher-ups. If someone walks in late, it can disrupt the flow and make it hard for others to focus. Now, do I think this rule is necessary? Absolutely! Being punctual shows professionalism and commitment. It sets a positive tone for the meeting and helps build a culture of accountability. 

Of course, life happens, and sometimes delays are unavoidable, but overall, sticking to this rule fosters respect and productivity. Plus, it kind of encourages everyone to be more organized in their day-to-day tasks, which is a win-win for the entire team! So yeah, I’d say it’s definitely a must-follow.

Oh, sure! So, there was this talk I attended not too long ago, and it really blew my mind. It was given by a climate activist who had a knack for storytelling. You know how some speakers just have that presence? Well, she totally did. She started by sharing her personal journey, which just hooked everyone from the get-go.

She talked about growing up in a coastal town and how rising sea levels were a real threat to her community. It was super eye-opening because, honestly, I never thought about how climate change affects people on a personal level like that. She went on to discuss some innovative solutions, like using sustainable energy sources and community-driven initiatives. It got me thinking about what we can do in our own lives.

What really stuck with me, though, was when she said, "Change starts small, but it ripples out." It's such a simple idea, but it felt so powerful. It made me realize that even tiny actions can lead to bigger changes. Overall, that talk was not just informative but also incredibly inspiring, and I've been trying to get more involved ever since.

Well, you know, TV shows and movies can be really powerful tools for teaching people important lessons. I mean, they’re not just for entertainment; they can also convey some pretty deep messages. For instance, a lot of shows tackle social issues, like racism or mental health, in ways that resonate with viewers. When people see these stories play out on screen, it can spark conversations and make them think about their own perspectives.

Plus, there’s something about visual storytelling that sticks with us, right? When you see characters go through challenges and grow, it kind of connects you to their journey. It makes those lessons feel more real and relatable. Take movies like “The Pursuit of Happyness,” for example. That film really highlights resilience and determination. You can’t help but feel inspired after watching it!

And let’s not forget about documentaries. They’re great for educating people about real-world issues, giving an in-depth look at things we might not know much about. So yeah, in a nutshell, TV shows and movies can serve as a mirror to society, helping folks learn and reflect while being entertained at the same time. It’s pretty amazing when you think about it!
title: How can TV shows and movies help teach people important things?

answer:
One thing that really comes to mind is learning a new language. Honestly, it can be quite a daunting task at first, right? I mean, there’s so much to take in—grammar rules, vocabulary, pronunciation, and all that jazz. It feels like you’re just drowning in information! But, at the end of the day, it’s totally worth it. 

Once you start getting the hang of it, you realize how enriching it is to connect with people from different cultures. There’s something magical about being able to chat with someone in their native tongue. And let's not forget the travel opportunities! Imagine visiting a country where they speak that language and being able to dive deep into the local scene. You get a whole different experience compared to just sticking to English. Plus, it’s super impressive on a resume, too. Employers really love seeing language skills because it shows you’re adaptable and open-minded.

So, while it might take time and a lot of patience to reach fluency, the benefits are just out of this world. I think the journey really makes it all worthwhile in the end, you know?

Well, I remember this one time in high school when I had to give a presentation in front of my class. It was for my history project, and honestly, I was pretty nervous about it. You know how it is—standing up there in front of everyone can be super intimidating! 

So, I did my research on the Civil Rights Movement, which is a fascinating topic, but when it came time to present, I felt like my heart was racing a mile a minute. I started off okay, but halfway through, I stumbled over my words a bit. Thankfully, my teacher gave me a reassuring smile, and that kind of helped ease my nerves.

I made sure to engage the class by asking questions and sharing interesting stories about key figures like Martin Luther King Jr. and Rosa Parks. It really helped to see their interest; I could tell they were paying attention. By the end of it, I actually enjoyed the experience, and it felt great to have accomplished something that once seemed so daunting. The moral of the story? Facing your fears can lead to some rewarding moments!

Well, you know, balancing study and fun is super important for students. First off, hitting the books is crucial for gaining knowledge and acing those exams, but if you spend all your time studying, you might end up feeling burned out. No one likes that, right? So, taking breaks and doing fun activities can actually help refresh your mind. 

Plus, let’s be honest—having fun is what makes life enjoyable! Engaging in hobbies or hanging out with friends can boost your mood and give you a sense of fulfillment. It’s like recharging your batteries. When you’re happy and relaxed, you tend to be more productive when you do sit down to study. 

Another thing to consider is that college or school, while primarily about learning, is also about making memories and building relationships. Participating in social events or just chilling with friends helps you develop skills like teamwork and communication, which are super valuable down the line. 

So, in a nutshell, finding that balance between study and fun not only enhances your academic performance but also enriches your life experience. It’s all about enjoying the journey while striving for success!

Oh, you know, looking at art online has really become a thing over the past few years, especially with everything that’s been going on. I mean, it’s super convenient, right? You can just hop on your computer or even your phone and browse through thousands of pieces from all over the world without leaving your couch. It’s like having a personal museum at your fingertips!

But, honestly, there’s something kinda magical about seeing art in person that you just can’t replicate online. The details, the texture, the colors—those elements really pop when you stand right in front of the piece. Plus, there’s the whole vibe of being in a museum or gallery, surrounded by other art lovers. I mean, there’s a certain energy in the air that's hard to describe.

That said, online art viewing does offer an opportunity to discover artists and styles you might not encounter otherwise, which is pretty cool. You can also read up on the background of a piece instantly, which adds another layer of understanding. So, while nothing quite beats the real thing, online art definitely has its perks too!

Sure! So, let’s talk about "To Kill a Mockingbird" by Harper Lee and "The Hate U Give" by Angie Thomas. Both books tackle really important social issues, like racism and injustice, which is why I find them quite similar. In "To Kill a Mockingbird," we see it through the eyes of Scout Finch, and it’s set in the 1930s. It paints a powerful picture of racial tensions in the South. On the other hand, "The Hate U Give" is more contemporary, focusing on police brutality and the Black Lives Matter movement.

Now, while both books address these heavy themes, they do it in different styles and contexts. "To Kill a Mockingbird" has this nostalgic feel, with a lot of focus on childhood innocence and moral growth, which I think really resonates with readers. Meanwhile, "The Hate U Give" feels much more urgent and raw, reflecting a modern-day struggle that many people can relate to right now.

So, to sum it up, they’re similar in their exploration of race and justice, but they differ in their perspectives and the eras they represent. Both are definitely worth a read, though!

answer:
Sure! One special day I really look forward to every year is my birthday. It’s just that day when everything feels magical, you know? Friends and family usually gather around, we have some cake, and there’s always laughter and fun. What I love most is the feeling of being celebrated; it’s like a little spotlight on me for a change! Plus, I get to make a wish when I blow out the candles, which is kind of exciting.

Now, the other special day I cherish is Thanksgiving. It’s totally different from my birthday in a lot of ways. Instead of being all about me, it's really about gratitude and coming together with loved ones. We have this big feast, and it’s honestly a time for everyone to share what they’re thankful for. I mean, there’s just something heartwarming about sitting around the table, enjoying delicious food, and connecting with everyone. So, while my birthday is more about personal celebration and fun, Thanksgiving is all about community and appreciation. Both days are special in their own way, but they definitely create very different vibes!
title: Tell me about two special days you enjoy and how they're different.

I think digital equipment can both help and hurt learning, but personally, I see more benefits than drawbacks. You know, when I think about the classroom, technology has really transformed how we access information. For example, using tablets or laptops during lessons allows students to research topics on the spot, which helps in developing critical thinking skills. I remember a time in history class when we were studying World War II, and our teacher encouraged us to find primary sources online. It made the topic feel so much more real and engaging.

However, there's definitely a downside too. Sometimes, I've noticed that students get easily distracted by social media or games when they're supposed to be focusing on their studies. I mean, it's so tempting to check your notifications instead of paying attention to a lecture. It can disrupt the flow of learning and make it harder to absorb the material.

But overall, I think if used correctly, technology can enhance the educational experience. For instance, interactive apps and online platforms for practicing languages or math can provide personalized feedback that traditional methods can't offer. So, while there are challenges, I believe that with proper guidance from teachers and a focus on responsible use, digital equipment can really enrich the learning environment. In the end, it's about striking that balance between leveraging these tools for educational advancement and keeping ourselves accountable not to get sidetracked.
title: Does digital equipment help or hurt learning? Share your views on classroom technology.

Oh, okay, so back in high school, I actually helped organize an environmental club, which was, um, mainly focused on promoting sustainability and raising awareness about climate change. I mean, it wasn’t super big at first, but the goal was really to get students involved and, you know, make small changes in our community—things like recycling campaigns or planting trees around campus.  

My role was kind of a mix—I worked on outreach, like spreading the word about events, and also helped plan activities, like, uh, clean-up drives and workshops. Honestly, one challenge we faced was just, you know, keeping people engaged long-term. A lot of students liked the idea but struggled to stay committed. So, we tried to make things fun and social, like adding competitions or group rewards, which, thankfully, helped a lot!

Oh, okay, so, um, I remember this one time I had to repair my bike—it was pretty frustrating but kind of satisfying in the end. So, the chain had come off and gotten jammed, and honestly, I wasn’t sure where to start at first. I mean, I’ve seen people fix stuff like that before, but when it’s your own bike, you know, you kind of feel more pressure to actually get it right.

Anyway, I started by flipping the bike upside-down, which, like, gives you better access to the chain and the gears. I ended up needing a wrench to loosen part of the gear mechanism, and some gloves because, well, the whole thing was, uh, greasy. After wiggling the chain back into place and tightening everything up, it was good as new! Honestly, fixing it felt pretty rewarding.
title:

What did you need to repair?
How did you start the repair process?
Were any tools required?

answer: Oh, well, I think paper is one of those everyday things that we kind of take for granted, you know? There are so many types—like printer paper, notebook paper, wrapping paper, and even cardboard, which is technically a type of paper. It’s used in tons of ways, like for writing, printing, packaging, or even creating art. I mean, for me, I use paper daily for jotting down notes or sketching ideas—it’s super versatile. And, honestly, recycling paper is pretty easy, as long as it isn’t mixed with, like, food waste or plastic. Plus, recycled paper helps reduce deforestation, which is, you know, really important nowadays. So yeah, paper plays such a huge role in our lives—it’s practical, creative, and even eco-friendly when we recycle it properly.

Well, I think doing research is definitely valuable, especially when it comes to homework. You know, it allows us to dig deeper into topics that interest us and really understand the material beyond just what's taught in class. For example, when I was working on a project about climate change, I found that researching different sources not only helped me ace my assignment but also sparked my passion for environmental issues. 

I mean, initially, I thought it would just be a time-consuming task, but I ended up discovering so much more than I expected. The critical thinking skills I developed and the ability to evaluate different viewpoints are things I truly value now. In today's world, where misinformation is rampant, being able to conduct thorough research is more important than ever. 

Also, research fosters creativity. Sometimes while looking for information, I stumble upon fascinating studies or articles that completely shift my perspective or inspire new ideas. This creative process is essential, especially as I prepare to apply to universities. It helps me articulate my thoughts better and express my opinions in essays.

That said, I can see how some may feel that research just adds extra stress, particularly if it feels overwhelming or unmanageable. However, I believe that with the right mindset, we can turn that stress into an opportunity for growth. So, overall, I find that the benefits of doing research far outweigh the time it takes.
title: Is doing research valuable homework or not worth the time? What's your view on this?

answer:
Honestly, I usually prefer reading fiction over nonfiction, and I guess there are a few reasons for that. First, I think fiction lets me escape from daily life and kind of explore different worlds or perspectives that I wouldn’t experience otherwise. For example, I remember reading “The Catcher in the Rye,” and honestly, I felt so connected to Holden Caulfield even though his life is totally different from mine. It’s like the author creates an entire universe that I can get lost in, which just doesn’t happen as much with nonfiction—at least for me.

But, you know, it’s not that I dislike nonfiction. I do find biographies or books about psychology pretty intriguing sometimes. Like, I read Malcolm Gladwell’s “Outliers” a while ago, and it actually made me reflect on how opportunities can shape someone’s life. Still, if I had to choose, I’d pick fiction most of the time because I really appreciate the creativity and imagination involved. Fiction books often challenge my perspective and help me empathize with characters going through situations I’ve never faced.

Also, I think fiction enhances my vocabulary in a subtle way since authors often use more expressive language. That’s helped me, especially when I’m preparing for university in the U.S. But, I mean, with nonfiction, although the information is valuable, it sometimes feels a bit monotonous or, like, too focused on facts rather than emotions.

So overall, I definitely lean toward fiction because I enjoy stories that spark my imagination and let me see things from new angles. That’s what keeps me motivated to read regularly and explore different genres.
title: What kind of books do you prefer to read--fiction or nonfiction? Explain your reasons for your preference.

A well-organized person is someone who demonstrates a high level of efficiency and orderliness in their personal and professional life. To stay organized, they may rely on various strategies such as creating to-do lists, setting priorities, using digital or physical planners, and maintaining a clean and clutter-free workspace. They also possess excellent attention to detail and are skilled at categorizing and organizing information.

answer:
Ok, international sports competitions, like the Olympics and World Cup, have long been regarded as a platform that brings people from different countries together. These events have the power to unite nations and transcend cultural and political differences, fostering a sense of camaraderie and global community. Through the shared experience of cheering for their respective teams, fans develop a common bond and learn to appreciate diverse cultures.

However, there are negative consequences associated with these competitions. The intense rivalry and competition can sometimes lead to aggression and violence among fans. Additionally, the immense pressure on athletes to perform at their best can result in doping scandals and unethical practices in pursuit of victory. Furthermore, the cost of hosting such mega-events can be exorbitant, straining the resources of the host country and neglecting other areas in need of development.

Looking ahead, sports competitions are likely to undergo significant changes. Advancements in technology, such as virtual reality and augmented reality, may enhance the viewing experience for fans who cannot attend the events in person. There might also be an increased focus on sustainability and inclusivity, with efforts to minimize the environmental impact of hosting competitions and promoting equal opportunities for participation irrespective of gender or disability.

In conclusion, international sports competitions have the potential to bring people together by fostering a sense of unity and shared experiences. However, they also have negative consequences such as violence and financial burden. Moving forward, sports competitions are expected to embrace technological advancements and prioritize sustainability and inclusivity.
title:

Do you believe they bring people together?
What are the negative consequences of these competitions?

answer:
Honestly, if I had to pick my least favorite school subject, I’d say it’s probably chemistry. I mean, don’t get me wrong, I know it’s really important and all, but I always felt kind of lost in those classes. There are just so many formulas and rules you have to memorize—like, half the time it felt more like doing math than actually understanding how things work. For me, what makes chemistry so unenjoyable is the way it’s usually taught. It’s mostly lectures and worksheets, and the experiments are pretty basic. You know, sometimes we would just mix two clear liquids and watch them turn blue, but I never fully understood why that was happening. It just didn’t feel engaging or relevant to real life.

If I could change anything about chemistry, I’d definitely make it more hands-on and connected to practical situations. For example, I think it would be awesome if teachers could bring in more interactive projects, like analyzing the chemicals in everyday food or maybe even something like testing water quality from our local community. That kind of approach would help students see the actual impact of what they’re learning, and it would keep things interesting. Also, I wish there were more opportunities for collaboration and discussion instead of only following instructions quietly. So, overall, while I know chemistry is essential, I just find it a little inaccessible. Making it more relevant and interactive would, I think, totally change the way students experience the subject.
title: What is your least favorite school subject? What makes this subject unenjoyable for you? How would you change the subject to make it more enjoyable?

answer:
Taking classes online has become increasingly popular in recent years, offering a range of advantages and disadvantages compared to traditional classroom learning.

One of the major advantages of online classes is flexibility. Students have the freedom to study at their own pace and schedule, allowing them to balance their education with other commitments such as work or family responsibilities. Additionally, online classes often provide access to a wide range of resources and materials, including recorded lectures and interactive exercises, enhancing the learning experience.

Another advantage is the opportunity for personalized learning. Online classes can be tailored to individual needs and learning styles, allowing students to focus on specific topics or areas of interest. Moreover, students can interact with instructors and peers from diverse backgrounds and geographical locations, fostering a global learning community.

However, online classes also have some disadvantages. The lack of face-to-face interaction and real-time discussions can limit the depth of understanding and collaboration among students. Additionally, technical issues or poor internet connections may hinder the learning process and create frustration.

When it comes to subjects that are less suitable for online learning, practical and hands-on courses like laboratory-based sciences, fine arts, or physical education may not be effectively conveyed through virtual platforms. These subjects often require physical presence and direct interaction with instructors and equipment to acquire necessary skills and knowledge.

In conclusion, while online classes offer flexibility and personalized learning, they may lack in-person interaction and are not suitable for all subjects. It is essential to carefully consider the nature of the subject before deciding to study it online.
title:

answer:
Well, one hundred years ago, people lived in a very different world compared to today. Life was simpler and less connected. Technology was limited, and most people relied on manual labor for their daily tasks. Communication was primarily done through letters and telegrams, making it slower and more time-consuming.

Unlike now, transportation was less advanced, with horses and carriages being the primary mode of travel. There were no airplanes or cars, so long-distance journeys took much longer. Access to education was also more limited, especially in rural areas.

People faced many difficulties in their lives. Healthcare was not as advanced, and diseases were more prevalent. Many families struggled with poverty and had to work long hours to make ends meet. Women had limited rights and opportunities compared to men, facing discrimination in many spheres of life.

While it's fascinating to learn about the past, I don't think I would have wanted to live during that time. Although life may have seemed simpler, the lack of modern amenities, healthcare, and opportunities for personal growth would have made it challenging. I am grateful for the advancements we have made over the past century, which have improved our quality of life in many ways.
title:

Would you like to have lived during that time?

answer:
One historical place that I know about is the Great Wall of China. Located in northern China, it stretches over 13,000 miles from the east coast to the west coast of the country. The Great Wall of China holds immense historical significance as it was built to protect China from invasions and attacks during different dynasties.

The construction of the Great Wall began over 2,000 years ago during the Qin Dynasty and continued throughout various dynasties until the Ming Dynasty. It served as a strategic defense system, providing a physical barrier against enemy forces.

The Great Wall not only played a significant role in protecting China but also symbolized the unity and resilience of the Chinese people. It is recognized globally as one of the most remarkable feats of engineering and architecture.

Today, the Great Wall of China is a popular tourist attraction and a UNESCO World Heritage Site. It attracts millions of visitors every year who come to admire its grandeur and learn about its historical importance.

answer: One time I failed to achieve a goal was when I tried to learn scuba diving during a boat trip. I had been really excited about it because I wanted to try something new and challenge myself. My goal was to get into the water and complete the beginner diving experience. However, things did not go as planned. Since it was boat diving, the waves were quite strong, and I got really seasick before I even entered the water. I felt dizzy, nauseous, and weak, so in the end, I was not able to go into the water at all. At first, I felt very disappointed because I had looked forward to it for a long time. I also felt a little embarrassed, because other people seemed fine while I was struggling. But after I calmed down, I realized that forcing myself would only make things worse and could even be unsafe. So I accepted the situation and reflected on why I failed. This experience taught me two important lessons. First, I should prepare more carefully before doing any sport or outdoor activity, especially something physically demanding. For example, I should learn about seasickness in advance and take medicine if necessary. Second, not every activity is suitable for everyone, and that is completely okay. Sometimes the smarter choice is to find a sport that fits your body and condition better. Since then, I have paid much more attention to preparation and to choosing activities that are right for me.
title: Describe a time when you failed to achieve a goal. How did you respond to the failure, and what did you learn from the experience?

answer:
Hmm, that's an interesting question. If I had to pick a wild animal as a pet, I think I'd choose a fox. Honestly, I’ve always found foxes really fascinating—their intelligence and playful nature kind of remind me of both dogs and cats, but they have this unique charm. Of course, taking care of a fox at home wouldn’t be simple, you know? First, I’d need to make sure my house is kinda like a safe and spacious environment for it. Foxes are super curious and energetic, so I’d probably have to create areas where it could explore and play. I imagine I’d need to install secure fences in the yard, and provide enrichment activities like puzzle toys or even little tunnels.

As for feeding, I know foxes have a pretty varied diet in the wild, including fruits, vegetables, and small animals, but obviously, I’d stick to high-quality pet food, maybe supplemented with fresh produce to keep it healthy. Also, I’d definitely consult a veterinarian who specializes in exotic animals to make sure I’m doing everything right.

If my friends came over and saw that I had a fox as a pet, I think most of them would be genuinely surprised—maybe even a bit skeptical at first. Some might worry that it’s dangerous, but once they saw how friendly and well cared for it was, I bet they’d be really impressed. Actually, I think having such an unusual pet could spark a lot of conversations and make my home sort of memorable for people. Overall, I’d love the experience, but I realize it would require a lot of responsibility and dedication.

answer:
One thing that definitely worries me is money and providing for my family. With two kids and my wife staying home to raise them, I feel a lot of pressure as the sole breadwinner. Even though I have a decent job, money just seems to get tighter and tighter. 

It worries me because I want my kids to have all the opportunities I didn't have growing up. I want them to be able to play sports, take music lessons, go to summer camps - all those enriching activities. But every time an expense like that comes up, I stress about how we'll pay for it. 

I also worry about being able to afford health insurance and save for all our retirement accounts. It feels like no matter how much I make, it's never enough. And if anything happened to my job, we'd be in real trouble.

To cope, I try to be smart with our budget. I search for deals and ways to save money around the house. We rarely eat out or splurge. But still, this underlying financial stress hangs over me. I want my kids to have a carefree childhood, yet I'm constantly worried about our bank account balance. It's exhausting feeling like no matter how hard I work, I can't get ahead and provide the life I want for my family. That's the worry that keeps me up at night.
title: What is something that you worry about? Explain why that one thing worries you and how you cope with it.

answer:
I think Everything Everywhere All at Once has a good shot at becoming a classic. It just came out last year, but I think it has the makings of a movie that will stand the test of time. 

For one thing, it's just so creative and original. The idea of traversing the multiverse and seeing how your life could have been different is such a cool concept. And the way they brought that to life through trippy visuals and seamless editing was really impressive. It felt new and innovative, not like your typical sci-fi movie.

But it's not just about fancy effects - the story and characters are what make it so great. Michelle Yeoh's performance was phenomenal. You feel her character's struggle to connect with her family and find meaning in her mundane life. It's a pretty relatable and human theme, even with all the zany sci-fi stuff.

And it's just so fun! The action sequences are badass, and it doesn't take itself too seriously. There are a lot of laugh-out-loud moments mixed in with the emotional drama. It's really well-balanced in tone. So many movies these days go too far in one direction. Everything Everywhere All at Once finds that sweet spot.

So, with its originality, heart, and fun factor, I think this is a movie that will have a long life. People will want to rewatch it and experience that wild multiverse adventure over and over. It feels fresh while still having a classic quality. That's why I think it will stand the test of time.

answer:
Well, an unspoiled place refers to a location that remains unaffected by human interference and maintains its natural beauty and ecological balance. It is characterized by pristine landscapes, abundant wildlife, clean air, and untouched ecosystems. Such places often showcase the breathtaking beauty of nature and offer a sense of tranquility and harmony.

Personally, I have been fortunate enough to visit some unspoiled places around the world. The untouched rainforests of the Amazon in Brazil, the vast deserts of Namibia, and the remote islands of the Galapagos in Ecuador are a few examples. These places left a lasting impression on me with their untouched natural wonders and rich biodiversity.

To protect unspoiled places, it is crucial to raise awareness about their importance and encourage responsible tourism practices. Governments and local communities should implement strict regulations to limit human impact, including controlling visitor numbers, enforcing waste management practices, and preserving fragile ecosystems. Education and sustainable development initiatives can help promote eco-friendly practices and reduce pollution. Furthermore, supporting conservation organizations and participating in volunteer programs can contribute to the preservation of these unspoiled places for future generations.

In conclusion, unspoiled places offer an unparalleled experience of the natural world. By prioritizing their protection through awareness, responsible tourism, and community efforts, we can ensure the preservation of these valuable environments and continue to enjoy their beauty.

answer: I believe one subject that most schools do not require, but should, is outdoor survival skills. In my opinion, this subject is valuable for every student because it teaches practical abilities that can be useful in real life, not just in the classroom. First of all, outdoor survival skills help students learn how to respond in emergencies. For example, they can learn how to find clean water, make a fire, read a map, or ask for help if they get lost. These may sound like simple skills, but in a dangerous situation, they could actually save someone’s life. In addition, this subject can make students more independent and confident. Many young people today spend most of their time indoors and depend heavily on technology. If their phone runs out of battery or they lose internet access, some of them may not know what to do. Learning survival skills would encourage them to stay calm, think clearly, and solve problems on their own. Another reason is that this subject can help students build a stronger connection with nature. By learning how to survive outdoors, they may also learn to respect the environment and understand how important it is to protect natural resources. In conclusion, I strongly believe outdoor survival skills should be taught in schools. It is a practical and meaningful subject that prepares students for unexpected situations and helps them grow into more capable and responsible adults.

answer:
In my opinion, the most useful household appliance in my home is the refrigerator. It is an essential appliance that keeps our food fresh and prevents it from spoiling. 

The refrigerator works by utilizing a cooling system. Inside the refrigerator, there are coils and a compressor that work together to pump refrigerant and remove heat from the air inside. This process lowers the temperature and keeps the contents cool. The refrigerator also has insulation to keep the cool air from escaping, ensuring efficient cooling.

Without a refrigerator, life would be much more challenging. We would have to rely on buying fresh groceries every day as there would be no way to store perishable items for an extended period. Our food would spoil quickly, leading to wastage and frequent trips to the grocery store. Additionally, we would not be able to enjoy chilled beverages or frozen treats. The convenience of having leftovers and being able to meal prep would also be greatly hindered. In essence, life without a refrigerator would be less convenient, more expensive, and involve more frequent trips to the store.

answer:
If I could change one thing about school, honestly, I think it would be the way schools measure success—like, the whole focus on grades and standardized tests. You know, for me, it always felt kind of limiting because these tests don't really reflect everything a student is capable of. For example, I remember having classmates who were super creative or amazing at problem-solving, but just because they didn't do well on certain exams, their abilities weren’t fully recognized. I mean, it’s frustrating when you realize how much talent goes unnoticed just because someone isn’t great at memorizing facts or performing under high-pressure test conditions.

So, if I could, I’d push for a more comprehensive way of evaluating students, maybe something that combines academic performance with practical skills, creativity, and collaboration. For instance, schools could implement more project-based assessments where students work on real-life scenarios or team projects. That way, people’s unique strengths would be more visible. Also, incorporating presentations or portfolio work alongside traditional exams would kind of help everyone showcase what makes them stand out.

Well, another reason I’d want this change is because, as someone applying to universities, I find that schools mostly look at grades and test scores, which makes the whole process super competitive and stressful. If we could shift the emphasis towards a broader set of skills, I think education would feel more meaningful and students could actually enjoy learning instead of feeling pressured all the time. So yeah, that’s definitely something I’d love to see in the future.

answer:
Honestly, I think studying our dreams isn’t really a waste of time—actually, it can be quite fascinating and even useful sometimes. You know, I’ve read that dreams can reflect what’s going on in our subconscious, like our fears, hopes, or even stuff we’re stressed about. For example, before my final exams last year, I kept having dreams about being late to class or forgetting my notes. At first, I thought it was just random, but then I realized it was probably connected to my anxiety about doing well academically. That made me pay more attention to how I was feeling during the day.

Also, psychologists like Freud and Jung have devoted entire theories to interpreting dreams, and while some ideas might sound a bit far-fetched, I do think there’s value in looking for patterns or meaning. Sometimes, dreams can help us process emotions or even find creative solutions to problems. I mean, I’ve heard musicians and artists say they got inspiration from something they dreamed about. So in that sense, understanding your dreams could lead to more self-awareness or even spark innovation.

Of course, not every dream means something profound—sometimes, it’s just random images or total nonsense. But if you take the time to reflect, you may discover certain recurring themes that reveal what's really on your mind. In my opinion, exploring dreams isn’t pointless. It’s a way to understand yourself better, and as someone about to start university, I feel like self-discovery is pretty important. So yeah, I definitely don’t see it as a waste of time.

answer:
I don't believe people can ever be completely selfless, but some can come close through their intentions and actions. True selflessness would mean having no personal interest or benefit from one's kind deeds. But the reality is that helping others often makes us feel good about ourselves, too.

Altruism lights up the same pleasure centers in our brains as food and money. Doing good releases endorphins and boosts our self-esteem. We feel satisfied when we make someone smile or meet their need. While some call this selfish, I see it as human nature - helping others makes us feel whole. Very few would continue giving if it left them empty inside.

However, some demonstrate immense selflessness by dedicating their whole lives to service. Think of Mother Teresa caring for the destitute or volunteers working thanklessly through disasters to rescue others. They act purely out of love and compassion, without expecting rewards or recognition. Their gratification comes from easing others' suffering. Their selfless spirit is aspirational.

Ultimately, true selflessness is rare. But in my view, the purity of motivation matters more than getting joy from giving. As long as we're not helping others solely to benefit ourselves, the warmth it kindles in our hearts is just part of being human. Our souls resonate when we act for good. If we give freely without obligation, we can still capture the spirit of selflessness.

So, while genuine selflessness may be impossible, that ideal can still guide us. When we give freely, listen intently, and act with compassion, we create a society where everyone feels supported - and that is an end in itself.

answer:
One time I was selfless was when I volunteered at a local homeless shelter during the holidays. It was a busy time of year, and I had a lot on my plate, but I felt compelled to help out. I spent a few hours each week at the shelter, helping to serve meals and chat with the guests. It was a small act, but it meant a lot to the people there.

My actions benefited the guests at the shelter, many of whom were going through tough times. There was one particular evening when an older gentleman came in looking really down. He sat by himself, and I could tell he was feeling lonely. I decided to sit with him and strike up a conversation. We talked about his life, his family, and his favorite holiday memories. By the end of the evening, he was smiling and thanking me for keeping him company.

His reaction was incredibly touching. He told me that talking to me made him feel less alone and more hopeful. It was a small act of kindness, but it made a big difference to him. He said it was nice to know that someone cared and that it gave him something to look forward to during a difficult time.

This experience taught me that selflessness doesn't have to be a grand gesture; sometimes, it's the small acts that have the most impact. It made me realize that taking the time to listen and show compassion can brighten someone's day and make them feel valued. It was a powerful reminder of the importance of empathy and the ripple effect of kindness.

answer:
Honestly, when I think about historical leaders who had a positive impact, Nelson Mandela comes to mind right away. I mean, there are a lot of good leaders out there, but Mandela is kind of iconic because of how he managed to unite people and promote equality after so many years of division in South Africa. What’s really impressive is that even after spending almost three decades in prison, he didn’t come out bitter or vengeful. Instead, he led the country towards reconciliation and pushed for democracy, which actually transformed the lives of millions of people.

For example, his work to end apartheid helped build a society where people have more freedom and rights—like, equal access to education and jobs, which wasn’t possible before. Also, I find that his ability to forgive and encourage others to move past old grievances is something that had a huge ripple effect, not only in South Africa but also as inspiration all over the world. You know, it takes a lot of resilience and vision to lead a divided nation toward unity.

From his example, I feel like I can learn the importance of perseverance and empathy. It shows that trying to understand other people's perspectives—even if they seem completely different or even antagonistic—is vital to creating real change. Plus, being able to stay hopeful and optimistic despite severe adversity is an essential leadership quality. So, yeah, Mandela’s life teaches me that genuine leadership is about putting others first, staying principled, and always striving for positive transformation.

answer:
Honestly, when it comes to coping with pressure, I think one of the most effective ways is just learning how to manage your mindset. I mean, I’ve found that a lot of the pressure I feel, especially while applying to universities, comes from my own expectations and worries about the future. For me, taking a step back and putting things into perspective really helps. Like, instead of obsessing over a single test or application, I remind myself that one moment doesn’t define my whole life. That kind of mentality is super important.

Also, talking to others plays a big role. You know, whenever I’m stressed, I try to reach out to friends or family—sometimes just saying what’s on your mind makes things seem less overwhelming. For example, before my final exams, I was honestly freaking out, but after chatting with a friend who was in the same boat, I realized I wasn’t alone, and we kind of supported each other through it. That sense of solidarity made the pressure much more manageable.

Besides that, I think having healthy outlets matters too. Personally, I go for walks or listen to music when I feel under pressure. It’s pretty amazing how something as simple as that can help you refocus and recharge. Well, everyone’s different, but for me, these strategies—shifting my mindset, connecting with others, and finding ways to decompress—have been essential. So, I really believe that coping with pressure is all about combining emotional awareness with practical actions.

answer:
Well, in my opinion, beliefs about luck vary across cultures and individuals. Some people believe in the concept of luck, considering it to be a force that influences their lives and outcomes. Others may perceive luck as random chance or dismiss it altogether.

Certain numbers are often associated with luck or superstitions in different cultures. For example, the number 7 is commonly believed to be lucky in many Western societies, while the number 8 is considered lucky in Chinese culture due to its pronunciation resembling the word for wealth. Conversely, some numbers like 13 are commonly considered unlucky in Western cultures.

Many individuals carry objects for good luck or as talismans. These objects can range from crystals, charms, or religious symbols to personal items with sentimental value. The belief in these objects providing protection or bringing good fortune varies from person to person and is often based on personal experiences or cultural traditions.

Traditions surrounding luck can be found in various countries. For instance, in China, the color red is associated with luck and is often used during festive occasions such as the Lunar New Year. In Ireland, it is believed that finding a four-leaf clover brings good luck. These traditions and beliefs are deeply rooted in cultural practices and are often passed down through generations.

Overall, beliefs about luck are subjective and vary greatly based on individual experiences, cultural backgrounds, and traditions. Whether one believes in luck or not, these beliefs add depth and diversity to our understanding of human perceptions and behaviors.

answer:
Honestly, I think this is a pretty complicated issue. On one hand, it's kind of obvious that robots and automation have already started replacing some jobs, especially in factories and places like that. For example, my uncle used to work in manufacturing, and he always tells me about how machines took over tasks that people used to do manually. So, yeah, there’s definitely a risk that some jobs might disappear because of robots. But at the same time, I mean, technology’s always created new opportunities too. If you look at how things have changed in recent years, there are all these new fields like robotics engineering, software development, and AI research. People are needed to design, program, and maintain these robots, you know?

Also, I feel like robots can actually make life easier by taking over tedious or dangerous tasks, which allows humans to focus on more creative or meaningful work. For instance, in hospitals, robotic assistants can help doctors and nurses with routine jobs, so medical staff can spend more time with patients. In a way, it kind of encourages innovation, since people have to adapt and learn new skills to stay relevant in the workforce.

Well, I wouldn’t say that robots will only cause job loss or only create jobs—it’s more of a shift. Some positions will probably vanish, but others will emerge. The key is staying adaptable and open to learning. Personally, I’m planning to study computer science because I think understanding technology is crucial now. So, while robots might change the job market, I believe they’ll also open up new possibilities if we’re willing to evolve.
title: Do you think that robots will result in people losing their jobs, or do you think that they will create more jobs? Provide reasons to support your opinion.

answer: Honestly, if I could invite any two people to have dinner with me, I think I’d choose Malala Yousafzai and Robin Williams. I mean, the idea of sitting down with both someone so courageous and another person who brought so much joy to the world is just, you know, kind of incredible. First, I really admire Malala for her resilience and advocacy for education. As a recent high school graduate applying to universities, her story inspires me to pursue my goals even when things get challenging. I’d love to ask her how she managed to stay optimistic after everything she experienced, and maybe get her perspective on how young people like me can contribute to global education. Also, talking to Robin Williams would be amazing because, well, comedy is such a powerful way to connect with people from all backgrounds. He seemed genuinely empathetic and found ways to make people laugh, even when life was tough. I’d want to hear about his creative process and maybe ask him how humor helped him navigate difficult times. I feel like both of them, in their own unique ways, represent hope and resilience. Dinner with these two would probably teach me a lot about empathy, perseverance, and the importance of staying true to yourself, no matter what obstacles you face. So, yeah, that’s why I’d pick them—because I think learning from their experiences would really help me grow both academically and personally as I start this new chapter in my life.
title: Name two people-living or deceased-who you would invite to have dinner with you. Why did you choose those specific people? What would you hope to learn from them?

answer:
Honestly, I think it’s kind of unfair that sports coaches are fired just because their teams lose games or competitions. I mean, there’s a lot more to being a coach than just winning, you know? Sure, their job is to help the team succeed, but nobody can guarantee victory every time—especially in really competitive environments. For example, I remember my high school’s soccer coach got replaced after a tough season, and everyone was pretty upset. The thing is, he worked super hard with us and encouraged a positive atmosphere, but sometimes the players weren’t motivated or there were injuries that made it almost impossible for us to win.

Also, firing a coach just because of a few losses can create a toxic culture where people feel like they’re always walking on eggshells. It puts a ton of unnecessary pressure on everyone involved, which honestly doesn’t help performance in the long run. In my opinion, it makes more sense to evaluate coaches based on their overall contribution—like how they develop players’ skills, their ability to foster teamwork, and their commitment to sportsmanship. These factors are, I think, just as crucial as the actual scoreline.

On the other hand, I get that professional sports are pretty ruthless, and sometimes results are all that matter to owners or fans. But even so, expecting coaches to be miracle workers isn’t entirely realistic. So, to sum up, I believe coaches shouldn’t automatically be dismissed for losing; their impact goes way beyond just wins and losses.
title: Do you think it's right that sports coaches are fired when their teams lose games or competitions? Provide reasons to support your opinion.

answer:
When I’m feeling creative, honestly, the first thing I do is grab my sketchbook and start drawing—sometimes it’s just random doodles, but other times I get really absorbed in designing characters or even imagining scenes from stories. Drawing isn’t just about making art for me; it’s kind of a way to unlock new ideas and see things from a fresh perspective. You know, when I start sketching, my imagination just opens up because I don’t really have any boundaries—I can invent things that don’t exist, play with different styles, or create entire worlds if I want. 

Also, I think that being creative like this encourages me to take risks, since there’s no right or wrong answer in art. It’s actually pretty liberating. For example, last summer, I was working on a project where I combined real-life photos with abstract drawings, which helped me experiment and become more innovative. My friends sometimes ask me how I come up with these ideas, and I always tell them that creativity is mostly about letting yourself make mistakes and not worrying too much about being perfect. 

If my friends wanted to improve their creativity, I’d suggest they try something outside their comfort zone—like writing a story or even collaborating on something artistic together. I mean, creativity grows when you’re open-minded and willing to explore. Honestly, the best thing I’ve learned is that imagination gets stronger the more you use it, so I encourage my friends to just start creating, no matter what the outcome is.
title: Name something you do when you are feeling creative. How does this activity open your imagination? What can your friends learn from you to improve their creativity?

answer:
One school activity I really enjoy outside of class is the debate club. I've been part of it since freshman year, and it's been a great experience. What I enjoy most is getting to research complex topics and learn about different perspectives. Coming up with quick, reasoned arguments is also fun - it forces you to think on your feet. When I have a debate, I feel challenged to make my points intelligently and persuasively. 

Our club meets twice a week to prepare for tournaments with other schools. We broke into teams and researched evidence on that month's debate topic. Then, we practice giving speeches and rebuttals. It's great practice for public speaking and critical thinking. I always learn a ton by researching in-depth case files. My research skills have really grown over the years.

If I could improve one thing about the debate club, I think it would be to add more social time. With all the intense practice for tournaments, we don't get to relax with each other much. It would be nice to hang out more as friends outside of just preparing for debates. Maybe we could have pizza study breaks or meet up outside school to get to know each other better. 

Overall, though, I'm really glad I joined the debate club. It's taught me so much, and I've made great friends who are interested in the same things. My confidence in presenting ideas and arguing respectfully has grown immensely, thanks to the club. I'd recommend debate to anyone looking to challenge themselves intellectually and improve their public speaking skills.
